grammar •  264  that-clauses can be followed by that-clauses. It is best to check  in a good dictionary.             -> Unfortunately, there is no easy way to decide which  nouns, verbs or adjectives not It's worth your visiting the art museum . (     It's worth that you visit-...) - It's essential that you visit the art museum.

we should be there.) not I understood the importance of our being there. (     .. the importance that - I understood his wish that we should be there.

I want you to have a wonderful time. (     I want that you'll have...) - I hope that you will have a wonderful time.

Some verbs, nouns or adjectives can be followed by that-clauses; some cannot.

5 that-clauses        after    verbs,    nouns     and   adjectives For reasons why  that-clauses are often moved to the ends of sentences, ►    267.

She made it clear that she was not interested. (     She made that she was not in bed surprised me.) It surprised me that he was still in bed. (More natural than That he was still It is often used as a preparatory subject or object for a   clause (►  268-269).

4 preparatory         it For cases when prepositions are dropped before that-clauses, ►    210.

In spite of the fact that she had three small children, he sent her to prison for He held her completely responsible for the fact that she took food without not (     ... paid no attention to that she had just...) The judge paid no attention to the fact that she had just lost her husband.

follow prepositions directly).

The fact also introduces that-clauses after prepositions (that-c lauses cannot (More natural than That Simon had disappeared didn't...) The fact that Simon had disappeared didn't seem to worry anybody.

The fact that she was foreign made it difficult for her to get a job. (     That she introduced by the expression the fact.

It is unusual for that-clauses to stand alone as subjects. They  are more often 3   the    fact     that    ...

The Minister is anxious that nothing should get into the papers.

I admire your belief that you are always right.

And many nouns and adjectives can be followed by that-clauses.

I regretted that I was not going to be at the meeting.
